title: Guest post: Climate change has caused one-fifth of Pine Island glacier retreat - Carbon Brief
gist: A new attribution study (Bradley et al. 2026, The Cryosphere) combining physical climate models with machine learning finds that human-caused climate change is responsible for roughly 4km, or about a fifth, of the Pine Island glacier's retreat since the 1940s, marking the first-ever attribution study of Antarctic glacier retreat and directly linking greenhouse gas emissions to ice-sheet decline.

themes.theme: First attribution study of Antarctic glacier retreat

insights.claim: A new study is the first attribution study of glacier retreat in Antarctica, finding climate change responsible for around 4km (roughly a fifth) of Pine Island glacier's total retreat since the 1940s.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: First-of-its-kind quantification separates natural variability from anthropogenic forcing in ice-sheet retreat, giving climate-risk modelers and reinsurers a more defensible attribution fraction for sea-level-rise forecasts tied to this glacier system.
insights.quote: The research, the first attribution study of glacier retreat on Antarctica, finds that climate change has been responsible for around 4km – roughly a fifth – of the glacier’s retreat.

insights.claim: Pine Island glacier and its neighbour Thwaites together are responsible for almost half of all Antarctic ice-sheet sea-level-rise contribution, and Pine Island alone accounts for a fifth of net ice loss from the West Antarctic ice sheet, which itself accounts for nearly all Antarctic ice loss over the past 40 years.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: Concentration of Antarctic sea-level-rise risk in just two glaciers means investors/insurers modeling coastal exposure should weight Pine Island/Thwaites monitoring data disproportionately versus broader Antarctic ice-sheet averages.
insights.quote: Alongside its neighbour, the Thwaites glacier, it is responsible for almost half the sea level rise caused by melting ice sheets in Antarctica.

insights.claim: The glacier's retreat began in the 1940s, coinciding with a strong El Niño event that pushed a pulse of warm water to the ice sheet, roughly 20 years before human-caused warming began increasing warm-water intrusion to the region in the 1960s.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: Establishes that natural climate variability (El Niño) initiated the retreat before anthropogenic forcing took over as a contributing driver — a nuanced causal timeline that tempers simplistic 'climate change caused it all' narratives while still confirming a meaningful human contribution.
insights.quote: climate change started to affect the melt rate in the region 20 years after the retreat had already been initiated

insights.claim: Sediment records show the Pine Island glacier rested on a seabed ridge about 30km ahead of its current position for hundreds of years until the 1940s, and the West Antarctic ice sheet has retreated roughly 500km since the last glacial maximum 20,000 years ago, with most of that retreat occurring between 10,000 and 20,000 years ago.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: Establishes the long-run natural baseline of ice-sheet retreat (stable for ~10,000 years until the 1940s), which is the counterfactual against which the recent, climate-linked acceleration is measured — key context for judging how anomalous current retreat rates are.
insights.quote: Sediment records beneath the Pine Island glacier reveal that, for hundreds of years until the 1940s, the glacier rested on a seabed ridge that is about 30km ahead of where it sits today.

themes.theme: Methodology: hybrid physical models plus machine learning

insights.claim: Researchers used a novel hybrid method combining physical ice-sheet/ocean models with machine learning to reconstruct 250 years of glacier retreat, running many model simulations, validating against satellite and sediment-record data, then using machine learning to interpolate the full space of plausible model settings that simulation time constraints prevented running directly.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: A reusable framework (physical model ensembles plus ML emulation to fill computationally infeasible simulation gaps) that could be applied to Thwaites and other poorly-observed glaciers, expanding the addressable set of ice systems for which attribution and sea-level-rise risk can be quantified.
insights.quote: we used machine learning to identify relationships between model settings and simulated glacier retreat

insights.claim: Attribution science compares model simulations of the real, climate-changed world against a 'counterfactual' world without human-caused warming; for Antarctic ice sheets this requires running models at least 200 years into the past because ice sheets respond very slowly to climate changes.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: Explains why ice-sheet attribution lagged far behind attribution of heatwaves/droughts/wildfires (which need much shorter model runs) — signals more such long-run cryosphere attribution studies are now becoming feasible and may reshape sea-level-rise risk pricing.
insights.quote: To create these two modelled worlds in an Antarctic context, scientists need to run historical models for at least 200 years into the past.

insights.claim: A key source of uncertainty in the study is that pre-satellite-era sediment records reveal where ice was grounded (its footprint) but not how much ice volume was actually present, making initial model conditions uncertain.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: Flags a specific, named data gap (ice volume vs. footprint) that future research needs to resolve to tighten confidence intervals on the attribution fraction — a concrete signpost for what evidence would move this estimate up or down.
insights.quote: Although the sediment records tell us where the ice was grounded – that is, what its footprint was – they do not tell us exactly how much ice there was.

insights.claim: The study finds Pine Island glacier likely would have retreated even without climate change, just less far — analogous to how extreme weather events like drought or heavy rainfall could still occur without climate change, just with lower frequency or intensity.
insights.speaker: Dr Alex Bradley
insights.so_what: Reinforces the now-standard attribution-science framing (climate change as a risk multiplier, not sole cause) being extended from weather events to slow-moving ice-sheet dynamics, useful for communicating nuanced risk to investors who may otherwise expect binary attribution claims.
insights.quote: the Pine Island glacier may have retreated even without climate change, just not as far
